input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The exemplar focuses on a lab‑scale gantry crane operated within a research laboratory environment. The physical system consists of the mechanical gantry crane—comprising a horizontal gantry rail, vertical lift carriage, and a motorized or manual actuator platform—powered and controlled by an embedded controller that interfaces with stepper or servo drives and feedback encoders. This compact, Cartesian‑motion system is designed for educational and research applications, supporting small payloads and precise positioning. Its simple, well‑defined kinematics and readily available sensor interfaces make it ideal for building a corresponding digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin offers monitoring, validation, optimization, and visualization services: 1) Continuous data logging of the crane’s real‑time states (position, velocity, encoder readings) for historical analysis; 2) Continuous validation that compares live telemetry against the kinematic model to detect model drift or deviations; 3) Optimal control and trajectory planning based on the kinematic model to generate efficient, safe motion requests to the physical controller; 4) Fault‑detection support that utilises sensed data and model predictions to flag abnormal behaviour; and 5) Real‑time 3‑D and 2‑D visual renderings of the crane’s state, trajectories, and validation metrics. These services are targeted at researchers, educators, and system developers who wish to evaluate control strategies, validate models, and prototype predictive maintenance routines.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The digital twin implementation relies on freely available, commonly used software tools and libraries. It uses open‑source CAD packages to generate the crane geometry and an open‑source kinematic model library to represent the mechanical degrees of freedom. Data logging is performed through open‑source database or flat‑file storage (e.g., SQLite or CSV). Real‑time visualization is achieved with open‑source plotting and 3‑D rendering libraries. Interfacing with the physical controller occurs over standard serial or Ethernet protocols. The entire stack is deliberately built with generic, widely available components to ensure reproducibility and ease of adoption for researchers and students.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The GreenhouseDT exemplar adopts a centralized orchestration model where the twin logic runs inside a virtual machine environment for easy deployment. Sensor readings from the greenhouse are published to an ActiveMQ message broker, which publishes the data asynchronously to the twin services. These services then write time‑series measurements into an InfluxDB database for efficient historical analysis. Coordination between the data ingestion layer, simulation module, and behavioral adaptation controller is achieved through broker topic subscriptions, ensuring that state updates propagate instantly across the twin stack. The overall design leverages containerization within the virtual machine images, simplifying scaling and versioning of the twin components.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is facilitated through the Microsoft Azure cloud platform, which is employed to funnel sensor data from a Raspberry‑Pi sensor node into the Air Quality Management pipeline. The Azure ecosystem (IoT Hub, Functions, and possibly other services) orchestrates data ingestion, simulation, and prediction tasks. The digital twin itself is represented using AutomationML, a standardized XML‑based plant description format, allowing interoperability with other engineering tools and enterprise systems. External communication with the twin is performed via standard HTTP/REST interfaces and agent protocols (AMQP/JMS for ActiveMQ, HTTP line protocol for InfluxDB), ensuring compatibility with a broad range of enterprise IT infrastructures.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The physical crane is interfaced by a Python program (Physical Crane Interface) that reads sensor data such as position, velocity, load weight and swinging angle; the Logging Application then continuously writes these telemetry records into a TimescaleDB database. Data is transmitted via the crane’s dedicated controller communication (not explicitly specified, but exposed by the Python interface) and recorded at a fine‑grained, continuous cadence; event triggers are the execution of control commands or periodic controller polling. No explicit data quality or preprocessing steps are documented, and integration into the system occurs via standard SQL inserts into the TimescaleDB schema.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands originate from the Control Application, a Python script that constructs motion plans (e.g. a trajectory from the trajectory generator) and sends them to the Physical Crane Interface. The command payload format is not defined in the documentation, but it is communicated to the hardware controller through the same Python interface used for telemetry. Validation, authentication, conflict resolution, and execution confirmation are not described in the provided material.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Service inter‑communication relies on an MQTT broker that publishes and subscribes topics for state updates, trajectory commands, and validation results. The broker enables decoupled messaging among the Logging Application, Trajectory Generator Service, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service, and the dashboard. The networking environment is a local area network (LAN), with message transport over TCP/IP using MQTT. Bandwidth, latency, reliability, and security provisions (e.g., TLS, authentication) are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: All digital‑twin components are deployed on an on‑premise edge infrastructure. The Python applications (Control, Logging, Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Validation) run locally and share a common TimescaleDB instance. Computational requirements are implied by real‑time trajectory generation and simulation, but explicit CPU/memory or storage quotas are not presented. The architecture supports a single‑node deployment with potential to scale horizontally for additional cranes, although scalability mechanisms are not discussed.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process follows a repository‑centric workflow, with all source code, configuration, and Virtual Machine images maintained in a GitHub repository. Deployment for the GreenhouseDT exemplar uses virtual machine images pre‑loaded with prerecorded data, the InfluxDB time‑series database for sensor data storage, and an ActiveMQ broker to forward event messages between the physical greenhouse and the twin. The system is containerised or virtualised, enabling quick provisioning and scaling in either on‑premises or cloud environments. In contrast, the Air‑Quality‑Management exemplar gathers data from a Raspberry‑Pi equipped with temperature, humidity, CO₂, and particulate matter sensors, transports the streams to Microsoft Azure IoT Hub, and persists them in Azure Table or Cosmos DB. The digital model is encoded in AutomationML and deployed on Azure where simulation and predictive analytics (e.g., virus‑spread models) run. The overall pipeline supports continuous integration and deployment through automated container builds and VM provisioning, and the Git history provides version control and auditing of changes.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The GreenhouseDT offers real‑time runtime visualisation of greenhouse state, simulation of environmental dynamics (temperature, humidity), and predictive analytics to forecast future conditions, thereby enabling operators to adjust climate control settings. The Air‑Quality‑Management exemplar provides real‑time dashboards of indoor air quality metrics, simulation of airflow and contaminant dispersion, and probabilistic predictions for virus‑spreading risk, informing ventilation strategies and occupancy limits. Decision‑support flows from model outputs displayed on dashboards to manual or automated actuator commands; confidence scores or uncertainty bounds are not explicitly reported in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Air‑Quality‑Management exemplar adopts AutomationML as the digital twin model representation, aligning with the established plant engineering data‑exchange standard. The overall stack employs Microsoft Azure IoT Hub, which implements and interoperates with industrial IoT protocols such as MQTT, AMQP, and OPC UA, thereby assuring connectivity compliance with common industrial standards. Other standard compliance or certifications (e.g., ISO 23247, ISO 23246, SAE E31) are not mentioned in the provided documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses

4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.
